message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this lever. Default refuted=true if uncertain. Check the artifacts at /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board and the code at /home/polaris/wt/flywheel.

LEVER: Grammatical subject-position in-prose attribution (the ONLY cleaner-surviving form) (L2)
RUBRIC CRITERION: "Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized" (w=0.15 of comprehensiveness) + "Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles'" (w=0.15 of instruction)
CHANGE: The brief's deterministic marker->parenthetical transform is DEAD (probe variant B: cleaner retained zero authors/venues/years, byte-identical to the [n] baseline; every '(YYYY)' is deleted 281->0). Only probe variant C survives: author as grammatical subject — 'Acemoglu and Restrepo, writing in the *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, show that...'. So this is a WRITER-PROMPT change, not a post-pass: add a rule to SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_FIELD_AGNOSTIC that when an evidence row carries verified authors+venue, the finding is written with the author as subject/possessive and the venue as an in-clause appositive; do NOT spend tokens on years (100% deletion rate). Keep [ev_XXX] markers (stripped harmlessly). Post-verify: every injected author/venue token must be a byte-substring of the enriched sidecar record for that evidence_id, else strip the clause or drop the sentence.
WHERE: composer — /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:3764 (SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_FIELD_AGNOSTIC, new rule after rule 2 at ~:3770); verifier extension beside provenance_generator.py:2060 (author-bind helper) and compose script :104
CLAIMED: +0.008 to +0.012 overall (capped: the reference article itself has near-zero in-prose attribution and dalpha scores 0.5252 with bare surnames — this pays into ~4.4% of overall, not the brief's imagined 6+%)
ADMITTED FAILURE: The LLM writes attributions for rows lacking metadata (fabrication — must be caught and stripped by the byte-substring verifier); or it writes them in appositive/parenthetical positions the cleaner still deletes (cellcog's own Table 1 citation column was emptied); or attribution density reads as name-dropping and taxes readability L1.
CONTRACT: Two-lane contract, zero-fabrication preserved by construction. LANE A (world-facts): unchanged — every sentence asserting a fact about the world carries [ev_XXX] tokens and passes verify_sentence_provenance (provenance_generator.py:2098) with the NEUTRAL clause (entailment_judge.py:588) intact. LANE B (adjudication): a sentence tagged [syn] by the writer is exempt from span entailment and admitted iff it introduces NO new fact — enforced by a new verify_synthesis_sentence beside the existing gate: (i) DETERMINISTIC closed-world check: every digit-bearing token, proper noun, and named mechanism in the [syn] sentence must already appear verbatim in this section's ADMITTED lane-A prose or the bibliography (cellcog's winning synthesis — 'The three frameworks are complementary rather than competitive... layered lenses to be deployed according to the question at hand' — passes this check by inspection: no new number, entity, or fact; only ranking/relating ideas already on the page); (ii) FAIL-CLOSED LLM check: 'does this sentence assert any empirical claim about the world not already asserted in the FACTS block above, or does it only rank, relate, reconcile, or evaluate them?' — any parse error, timeout, or uncertain verdict = drop the sentence. LANE B-NEG (negative-space claims): sentences of the form 'no peer-reviewed study in this corpus has yet X' are claims about the CORPUS, not the world, and are verified mechanically against the evidence pool + bibliography (they are also immune to the NEUTRAL clause by construction — they introduce no mechanism into any span). ATTRIBUTION: author/venue strings injected into prose must be byte-substrings of the ENRICHED sidecar record for the cited evidence_id (extending _audit_citations at compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:104), and the sidecar itself is spot-verified against source pages before any compose — the current sidecar fabricates venues ('Review of AI', wrong years) and MUST NOT be injected until repaired. Red-team acceptance: a 30-sentence set (15 genuine adjudications, 15 with one smuggled fact/number/entity each) must show >=12/15 genuine admitted and 0/15 smuggled admitted, else Lane B ships disabled and we fall back to lane-A-only compose.

ATTACK THROUGH THIS LENS:
JUDGE-INVISIBILITY: RACE deletes all [n] markers and the reference list, then scores our report SIDE BY SIDE against a 0.5102 reference in ONE call. Would the judge SEE this, and would it push a criterion from the "6-8 Good" band into "8-10 Excellent"? There is no credit for being a better 7.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

[
 {
  "sev": "CRITICAL_serializes",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 (and :5514)",
  "title": "The 0615bc5 missing-await bug EXISTS AGAIN at a hotter call site: _compose_section_per_basket is a sync def called with no await/to_thread from the async _run_section \u2014 it blocks the event loop for the ENTIRE per-basket compose of a section",
  "mechanism": "`_compose_section_per_basket` is defined as a plain `def` (verified_compose.py:3262), not `async def`. `_run_section` (multi_section_generator.py:5182) is `async def`. At :5468 it is called as `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(...)` \u2014 no `await`, no `asyncio.to_thread`. This function is the MAIN per-basket compose loop: it walks every basket in the section, runs writer_fn + verify_fn + the bounded repair loop (redraft_fn) for each. All of it executes synchronously ON the asyncio event loop. Consequence: while section A is inside it, the single event loop is pinned \u2014 section B's coroutine cannot resume, and no in-flight writer/judge HTTP response can be processed. PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 is therefore fiction: the two 'parallel' sections are serialized. The same file already uses `await asyncio.to_thread(strict_verify, ...)` at :5667 and :10288, so the authors knew the pattern \u2014 :5468 and :5514 were simply missed, exactly as _draft_passes_wrapper was.",
  "fix": "Wrap both call sites: `_vc_composed = await asyncio.to_thread(functools.partial(_compose_section_per_basket, _vc_baskets, evidence_pool, writer_fn=..., verify_fn=..., redraft_fn=..., ...))`. This is the identical remedy applied in 0615bc5. Note it must be done TOGETHER with the redraft fix below, otherwise the offloaded thread still spins a fresh loop per redraft."
 },
 {
  "sev": "CRITICAL_serializes",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:4994",
  "title": "_redraft spins a FRESH asyncio event loop per call, which makes get_semaphore() destroy and recreate the module-global LLM concurrency semaphore \u2014 303 times in one run. PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM is not enforced at all.",
  "mechanism": "`_redraft` (the group re-draft closure fed into _compose_one_basket's bounded repair loop) is a sync function. At :4993-4994 it creates a new `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)` and does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))` \u2014 a BRAND NEW event loop for every single re-draft. Inside `_run()`, `_call_writer` -> `OpenRouterClient._call_api` (openrouter_client.py:1890) calls `get_semaphore()`. get_semaphore (llm_provider.py:149) guards on `_LLM_SEMAPHORE_LOOP is not running_loop` and, on mismatch, REBINDS the module-global `_LLM_SEMAPHORE = asyncio.Semaphore(cap)`. Because the redraft loop is never the main compose loop, every alternation main-loop -> redraft-loop -> main-loop destroys the semaphore TWICE. Permits held by calls already in flight on the old object simply vanish; the fresh semaphore starts at full cap. So the global cap of 30 bounds nothing, and burst concurrency is unbounded \u2014 which is what produces the 429/connection-error storm. Separately, `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` at :4996 is a BLOCKING wait of up to 330s, and because of finding 1 it executes on the event loop.",
  "fix": "Make _redraft async (`async def _redraft`) and await `_call_writer` directly on the caller's loop \u2014 the ThreadPoolExecutor+asyncio.run sandwich exists only because the caller chain is sync. If it must stay sync, pass the running loop in and use `asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe(_run(), main_loop)` instead of `asyncio.run(_run())`. Independently, harden llm_provider.get_semaphore: key the semaphore in a `WeakKeyDictionary[loop -> Semaphore]` instead of clobbering one module global, so a second loop cannot destroy the first loop's cap."
 },
 {
  "sev": "CRITICAL_serializes",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/llm/judge_concurrency.py:178 (acquired at src/polaris_graph/llm/entailment_judge.py:150)",
  "title": "Every NLI judge POST is gated by a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) \u2014 acquired ON the event loop thread",
  "mechanism": "`_post_with_total_deadline` opens with `with _acquire_judge_slot():` BEFORE submitting the POST. `acquire_judge_slot` resolves PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, which is set in NO launch script, so it falls to DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4, and does a blocking `sem.acquire()` on a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore. Two consequences: (a) at most 4 entailment POSTs are in flight process-wide no matter that PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 and PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 \u2014 the fixed pre-pass's 12 verify threads immediately queue 8-deep behind a cap of 4, so the 0615bc5 speedup is itself capped; (b) on the compose leg (finding 1) the thread doing sem.acquire() IS the event loop, so slot-queueing time is loop-freeze time on top of POST time.",
  "fix": "Set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY explicitly in the launch script (it was sized at 4 for a 4-host mirror chain during a 429 storm; with the compose leg off-loop, raise it to match PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM and watch 429 rate). Independently: never call acquire_judge_slot from the loop thread."
 },
 {
  "sev": "HIGH",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/llm/entailment_judge.py:1166 (and :1209)",
  "title": "time.sleep() rate-limit backoff (15-60s) inside the judge retry runs on the event loop thread",
  "mechanism": "`_EntailmentJudge.judge()` (:740) is fully synchronous. On a retryable fault it computes `_sleep_s = _rate_limit_backoff_s(...)` and calls `time.sleep(_sleep_s)` \u2014 not asyncio.sleep. For a 429/503/DNS fault the backoff is floored at PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S=15.0 and capped at PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_CAP_S=60.0, with a LARGER retry budget than the ordinary path. Reached from the compose leg (finding 1), that time.sleep executes on the asyncio event loop: one 429 on one sentence freezes the ENTIRE report generation \u2014 all sections, all pre-pass writer calls \u2014 for 15-60s, repeatedly. The module's own docstring says 429 storms on the mirror-chain lead host are the expected steady state.",
  "fix": "Getting the compose leg off-loop (finding 1) makes these sleeps block a worker thread, which is correct. Do not leave any path where judge() can be entered from the loop thread."
 },
 {
  "sev": "CRITICAL_serializes",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 (2nd call site :5514); loop body verified_compose.py:3325",
  "title": "_compose_section_per_basket is a SYNC blocking basket loop called with no await/to_thread from the async _run_section - it serializes every per-basket NLI judge AND freezes the whole event loop (all other sections)",
  "mechanism": "_run_section is `async def`. At :5468 it calls `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(...)` as a plain function call. inspect.iscoroutinefunction(_compose_section_per_basket) == False. Its body loops `for basket in (section_baskets or [])` (verified_compose.py:3325) and, per basket, calls _compose_one_basket / compose_basket_multicited_synth_primary / compose_companion_figure_units (nested member x sentence-unit loop at :3414), each of which calls the SYNC verify_fn(sentence, scoped_pool) (verified_compose.py:2222, 2583, 2981, 3071, 3192) -> make_writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance (provenance_generator.py:2154, sync) -> entailment_judge.judge() (sync) -> _post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result(timeout) which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD. The calling thread is the asyncio event loop. So (a) all per-basket verification in a section runs one judge POST at a time, and (b) for the entire duration the event loop is frozen, so every OTHER section's writer tasks, timeouts and HTTP callbacks make ZERO progress. This is exactly the 0615bc5 bug class, one level up, unfixed.",
  "fix": "Wrap both call sites in `await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, ...)` (unblocks the loop immediately, same as 0615bc5), then go further: make the per-basket loop fan out - either an async _compose_section_per_basket that gathers per-basket coroutines under a semaphore, or a bounded ThreadPoolExecutor over baskets with a copied contextvars context (the pattern already written for strict_verify at provenance_generator.py:3676-3718). Baskets are independent; verdicts are order-independent and are reassembled by index."
 },
 {
  "sev": "CRITICAL_serializes",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5448-5804",
  "title": "The post-draft tail is ~85-88% of every section's wall clock and has NO fan-out at all - the parallel writer pre-pass is only ~12-15% of the work",
  "mechanism": "The execution graph per section is: parallel pre-pass (:5448, Semaphore(24)) -> then a strictly serial chain of five blocking/serial stages: _compose_section_per_basket (:5468), _repair_untokened_draft (:5575), _coherence_rewrite (:5591), strict_verify (:5667), sentence repair (:5693), optional regen + a SECOND strict_verify (:5775/:5804). None of these stages pipelines with the next, and none of them fans out over sentences/baskets. So raising PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY / PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM can only ever compress the 12-15% slice; the other 85% is immune to every concurrency knob in the launch scripts.",
  "fix": "Parallelize inside the tail (see the other findings: basket loop, strict_verify workers, coherence re-verify, sentence repair) and/or pipeline it: start the next section's pre-pass while the previous section's verify tail runs - which becomes automatic once the tail stops blocking the event loop."
 },
 {
  "sev": "CRITICAL_serializes",
  "where": "scripts/run_s5_i3.py:491 (sec_sema) and :588-597 (task creation order)",
  "title": "94% of the baskets live in ONE section coroutine ('Additional Corroborated Findings', 304-326 of 346), and it is scheduled LAST behind a Semaphore(2)",
  "mechanism": "plans=4 -> 5 sections. Sections 0-3 carry 15-18 baskets each; section 4 carries 304-326. sec_sema = Semaphore(PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS) = 2 (iter4/iter5 launchers) and tasks are created in plan order, so section 4 only acquires the semaphore after two of the four small sections complete - i.e. it STARTS ~50-90 minutes into the run. Because the section is the unit of parallelism and its whole compose+verify tail is the single-threaded loop of finding #1, 94% of the work has an achieved parallelism of exactly 1. PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS cannot help: there is no second big section to overlap with.",
  "fix": "Two independent fixes: (1) shard the mega-section's baskets into N chunks composed by N concurrent tasks (the basket loop is already order-stable and dedup is done on the concatenated list, so chunking + in-order reassembly is safe); (2) schedule sections longest-first (sort tasks by len(section.ev_ids) descending) so the mega-section starts at t=0 and overlaps with all the small ones instead of being tail-scheduled."
 },
 {
  "sev": "HIGH",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/generator/provenance_generator.py:3664-3676 (gate) / :3383-3392 (default)",
  "title": "strict_verify's per-sentence entailment loop runs SERIAL because PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY is never set - the bounded thread pool exists and is dead code in production",
  "mechanism": "_parallel_verify_workers() returns 1 when PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY is unset/empty (:3390-3392). strict_verify then takes the serial branch at :3666-3676 ('if _verify_workers < 2 ... byte-identical to the legacy loop'), running one blocking entailment-judge POST per sentence, sequentially. The ThreadPoolExecutor path at :3678-3718 - which already handles contextvars copying and cost-ledger reconciliation - is never entered. strict_verify is invoked twice per section (:5667 and, on regen, :5804). grep shows PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY appears in NO launch_compose_gear_*.sh and in no os.environ.setdefault in run_s5_i3.py (which does setdefault 5 other flags at :342-359).",
  "fix": "export PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY=8 (or setdefault it in run_s5_i3.py next to the other Fix-3/Fix-6 setdefaults at :342-359). Zero code change; the parallel path is already written, tested and cost-reconciled."
 },
 {
  "sev": "HIGH",
  "where": "src/polaris_graph/generator/section_polish.py:271-283",

TASK 72 PROMPT:
Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

DIMENSION WEIGHTS: {"readability": 0.14, "insight": 0.32, "comprehensiveness": 0.29, "instruction_following": 0.25}

=== COMPREHENSIVENESS (weight 0.29) ===
- [w=0.10] Grounding in AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Assesses whether the review adequately defines AI within the context of the 4IR and explains its fundamental role as a driver of labor market restructuring. This contextual framing is crucial for understanding the scope and nature of the impacts discussed.
- [w=0.25] Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered :: Evaluates the extent to which the review addresses the multifaceted nature of AI's impact on the labor market, including job creation, job displacement, job transformation, changes in skill demands, wage effects, and productivity changes.
- [w=0.25] Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis :: Checks if the review examines the restructuring impact of AI across a diverse range of relevant industries, highlighting both common trends and sector-specific nuances. The task explicitly requires focusing on 'various industries.'
- [w=0.15] Exploration of AI's Disruptive Character and Scale :: Assesses whether the review sufficiently explores the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI, including the magnitude, speed, and transformative potential of these changes on labor market structures and dynamics.
- [w=0.15] Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized :: Evaluates if the review synthesizes a broad and representative selection of current, high-quality academic literature (from the specified journal articles), covering the main research themes, findings, and ongoing debates regarding AI's impact on the labor market. This ensures the review's content is built upon a solid and comprehensive foundation of existing knowledge.
- [w=0.10] Balanced Discussion of AI's Labor Market Impacts :: Assesses whether the review presents a nuanced perspective by discussing both the challenges (e.g., displacement, skill gaps, inequality) and opportunities (e.g., new job creation, productivity gains, enhanced work quality) arising from AI's influence on the labor market.

=== INSIGHT (weight 0.32) ===
- [w=0.25] Analytical Depth in Characterizing AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring Mechanisms :: Assesses if the review moves beyond superficial descriptions of labor market changes to deeply analyze the underlying *mechanisms* (e.g., task automation, skill augmentation, job creation/destruction dynamics, organizational adaptation) through which AI specifically reshapes job roles, skill requirements, and overall labor market structures.
- [w=0.25] Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation of AI's Disruptive Impacts Across Industries :: Evaluates the review's ability to critically synthesize diverse findings from high-quality journal articles to present a nuanced understanding of AI's 'significant disruptions.' This includes identifying patterns of impact, variations across different industries, and areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects.
- [w=0.15] Insightful Integration of AI's Role within the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Measures how effectively the review articulates and leverages the 4IR framework to provide a deeper, more contextualized understanding of the *nature, scale, and interconnectedness* of AI's transformative power on the labor market, rather than treating AI's impact in isolation or merely mentioning the 4IR superficially.
- [w=0.20] Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives :: Assesses whether the review transcends simple summarization to identify and clearly articulate overarching emergent themes, significant theoretical linkages between concepts, or potentially novel perspectives derived from the synthesis of the reviewed literature on AI's labor market impact.
- [w=0.15] Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: Evaluates the review's capacity to distill strategically valuable implications (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas based on the comprehensive synthesis of the current state of knowledge.

=== INSTRUCTION_FOLLOWING (weight 0.25) ===
- [w=0.10] Adherence to 'Literature Review' Format and Purpose :: Assesses if the article is structured and executed as a literature review, meaning it synthesizes and discusses existing published research on the topic, rather than presenting new empirical findings or being solely an opinion piece.
- [w=0.20] Consistent Focus on 'Restructuring Impact of AI on the Labor Market' :: Evaluates whether the article's content remains consistently and primarily focused on AI's restructuring effects on the labor market, as per the core task instruction, without significant digressions into unrelated AI applications or general economic theory not tied to labor impacts.
- [w=0.15] Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme :: Checks if the review explicitly incorporates and discusses AI within the specific context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, as mandated by the task's focus requirements.
- [w=0.15] Explicit Addressal of AI-Driven 'Significant Disruptions' in the Labor Market :: Assesses whether the review specifically addresses and elaborates on the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI in the labor market, fulfilling another key focus point of the task.
- [w=0.15] Coverage of AI's Impact on 'Various Industries' :: Evaluates if the review discusses the impact of AI on the labor market across a spectrum of 'various industries,' rather than limiting its scope to a single industry or providing only a generic overview, as per the instruction.
- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.

=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
- [w=0.20] L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone :: Evaluates the accuracy and fluency of sentence construction, grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Assesses the precise, consistent, and appropriate use of technical terminology related to AI, labor markets, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and specific industries. Ensures an objective, formal academic tone suitable for a literature review. For example, terms like 'skill-biased technical change' or specific AI techniques should be used correctly and, if necessary, briefly contextualized for a broader academic audience.
- [w=0.20] S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization :: Assesses the coherence and logic of the review's macro-structure. This includes a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map; logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor (e.g., by industry, skill level, type of disruption); and a conclusion that effectively synthesizes the main themes from the literature and perhaps points to gaps or future research directions.
- [w=0.15] S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions :: Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea or theme derived from the literature, with sentences flowing logically within the paragraph. Assesses the effectiveness of transitional phrases, sentences, and logical connectors in linking ideas smoothly between paragraphs and across different sections of the review, ensuring a coherent narrative thread.
- [w=0.15] P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information :: Assesses how clearly, concisely, and accurately key arguments, findings, theories, and methodologies from the cited journal articles are presented. Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics (e.g., AI's effect on job displacement vs. creation, or changes in skill demands across industries), rather than just serially summarizing individual papers. Checks for appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy.
- [w=0.10] D1: Clarity of Data/Evidence Referenced or Summarized :: Evaluates the clarity with which quantitative or qualitative data, empirical findings, or specific evidence from cited studies (e.g., statistics on automation rates, survey results on worker sentiment) are described, interpreted, or summarized within the review. If the review itself uses tables or figures to compile or synthesize information from sources (e.g., a summary table of key studies and their findings), assesses their clarity, labeling, and effectiveness in aiding comprehension.
- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.
- [w=0.10] A1: Audience Adaptation and Explanation of Terms :: Assesses if the language, level of detail, and style of argumentation are appropriate for an academic audience interested in AI's impact on the labor market. Evaluates whether potentially ambiguous or highly specialized terms are sufficiently contextualized or briefly explained to ensure understanding by readers who may not be experts in every specific sub-domain discussed (e.g., specific AI algorithms or niche economic theories).

（必要说明：中文互联网上流行的“九阶层/九等人”版本多为叙事文本，缺乏抽样与口径，不能当作统计分类；本文采用“**可与统计数据对接**”的分层方法：以官方收入与住户资产负债调查为“硬数据”，叠加职业—单位类型（体制内外）等结构维度来构造“九阶层画像”。）

---

## 1) 数据基础与关键口径（决定你看到的“中产人数/收入”差异）

### 1.1 收入：国家统计局住户收支调查（NBS）
国家统计局“住户收支与生活状况调查”是中国居民收入最核心的官方来源，约抽取**16万户**，分层、多阶段、PPS概率抽样，并通过记账与问卷统一汇总[[16]](https://www.stats.gov.cn/sj/zxfb/202501/t20250117_

cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board; python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,re,glob,os
models=[f for f in glob.glob('*.jsonl')]
# find task72 article per model, and its scores
def get(fn,i=72):
    for l in open(fn):
        d=json.loads(l)
        if d['id']==i: return d
    return None
sc={}
for m in models:
    name=m[:-6]
    s=os.path.join('scores',name,'raw_results.jsonl')
    row=None
    if os.path.exists(s):
        for l in open(s):
            d=json.loads(l)
            if d['id']==72: row=d
    a=get(m)
    if not a: print(name,'no72'); continue
    art=a['article']
    words=len(art.split())
    # in-prose attribution patterns after cleaner: surname-subject / "X (Year)" / "according to X"
    yearpar=len(re.findall(r'\(\s*(19|20)\d{2}',art))
    # Surname et al. / Surname and Surname as subject
    etal=len(re.findall(r'\b[A-Z][a-z]+ et al\.',art))
    andname=len(re.findall(r'\b[A-Z][a-z]+ (?:and|&) [A-Z][a-z]+\b',art))
    venue=len(re.findall(r'Journal of|Quarterly Journal|American Economic Review|Nature|Science\b',art))
    print(f"{name:35s} w={words:5d} scores={ (round(row['overall_score'],4), round(row['comprehensiveness'],4), round(row['instruction_following'],4)) if row else 'NA'} year()={yearpar:3d} etal={etal:3d} X_and_Y={andname:3d} venue={venue:3d}")
EOF

cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board; python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,re,glob,os
rows=[]
for m in glob.glob('*.jsonl'):
    name=m[:-6]
    s=os.path.join('scores',name,'raw_results.jsonl')
    if not os.path.exists(s): continue
    row=None
    for l in open(s):
        d=json.loads(l)
        if d['id']==72: row=d
    art=None
    for l in open(m):
        d=json.loads(l)
        if d['id']==72: art=d['article']
    if not row or not art: continue
    w=len(art.split())
    attrib=len(re.findall(r'\(\s*(?:19|20)\d{2}',art))+len(re.findall(r'\b[A-Z][a-z]+ et al\.',art))+len(re.findall(r'Journal of|Quarterly Journal|American Economic Review',art))
    rows.append((name,w,attrib,attrib/w*1000,row['overall_score'],row['comprehensiveness'],row['instruction_following'],row['readability']))
def spear(x,y):
    def rk(v):
        s=sorted(range(len(v)),key=lambda i:v[i]); r=[0]*len(v)
        for i,j in enumerate(s): r[j]=i
        return r
    a,b=rk(x),rk(y); n=len(x)
    return 1-6*sum((a[i]-b[i])**2 for i in range(n))/(n*(n*n-1))
rows.sort(key=lambda r:-r[4])
for r in rows: print(f"{r[0]:32s} w={r[1]:5d} attr={r[2]:3d} dens/1k={r[3]:5.2f} ov={r[4]:.4f} comp={r[5]:.4f} instr={r[6]:.4f} read={r[7]:.4f}")
d=[r[3] for r in rows]
for i,lab in [(4,'overall'),(5,'comp'),(6,'instr'),(7,'read')]:
    print(lab,'spearman vs attrib density:',round(spear(d,[r[i] for r in rows]),3))
# also vs raw word count
wl=[r[1] for r in rows]
for i,lab in [(4,'overall'),(5,'comp'),(6,'instr')]:
    print(lab,'spearman vs WORDS:',round(spear(wl,[r[i] for r in rows]),3))
EOF

content.content:
import json
import os
import time
import threading
import concurrent.futures
from tqdm import tqdm
from prompt.clean_prompt import clean_article_prompt_zh, clean_article_prompt_en
import logging

# Configure logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Shared LLM-call concurrency cap. All cleaning calls (any chunk of any article)
# acquire from this semaphore so total inflight LLM requests ≤ LLM_CONCURRENCY.
# Override via env LLM_CONCURRENCY=N. Default 20.
_LLM_CONCURRENCY = int(os.environ.get("LLM_CONCURRENCY", "20"))
_llm_semaphore = threading.Semaphore(_LLM_CONCURRENCY)
logger.info(f"LLM call concurrency cap = {_LLM_CONCURRENCY}")


class ArticleCleaner:
    def __init__(self, clean_agent):
        self.clean_agent = clean_agent
        self.min_valid_length = 100
    
    def _get_clean_prompt(self, language="zh"):
        """Return language-specific cleaning prompt template"""
        return clean_article_prompt_zh if language == "zh" else clean_article_prompt_en
    
    def _is_valid_result(self, text):
        """Check if cleaning result is valid"""
        return text and len(text.strip()) >= self.min_valid_length
    
    def _update_progress(self, pbar, pbar_lock):
        """Update progress bar safely"""
        if pbar and pbar_lock:
            with pbar_lock:
                pbar.update(1)
    
    def _is_token_limit_error(self, error):
        """Check if error is related to token limit"""
        error_str = str(error).lower()
        return "tokens" in error_str and "less than" in error_str
    
    _TRUNCATED = "__TRUNCATED__"

    def _clean_text(self, text, language="zh", max_retries=None):
        """Try to clean text. Returns string (incl. "") on success,
        self._TRUNCATED on output overflow, or None after all retries.
        max_retries kept for signature compatibility but the canonical budget
        is _RETRY_BACKOFFS_S (3 attempts with exponential backoff).
        """
        clean_prompt = self._get_clean_prompt(language)
        user_prompt = clean_prompt.format(article=text)
        backoffs = self._RETRY_BACKOFFS_S

        last_err = None
        for attempt, sleep_s in enumerate([0, *backoffs[:-1]]):
            if sleep_s:
                time.sleep(sleep_s)
            try:
                with _llm_semaphore:
                    result, stop_reason = self.clean_agent.generate(
                        user_prompt=user_prompt, system_prompt="",
                        return_metadata=True, stage="clean",
                    )
                if stop_reason in ("max_tokens", "length"):
                    logger.warning(f"Output truncated (stop_reason={stop_reason}), needs chunking")
                    return self._TRUNCATED
                # Accept any string (including "") — empty means the chunk
                # was entirely a reference list, per prompt contract.
                if result is not None:
                    return result
                logger.warning(f"Got None from API, attempt {attempt+1}/{len(backoffs)}")
            except Exception as e:
                last_err = e
                logger.error(f"API call error (attempt {attempt+1}/{len(backoffs)}): {e}")
                if self._is_token_limit_error(e):
                    logger.info("Article too long, needs chunking")
======PROMPT
# Based on a given research task and merged evaluation criteria list, compare two articles by each criterion
generate_merged_score_prompt = """
<system_role>You are a strict, meticulous, and objective research article evaluation expert. You excel at using specific assessment criteria to deeply compare two articles on the same task, providing precise scores and clear justifications.</system_role>

<user_prompt>
**Task Background**
There is a deep research task, and you need to evaluate two research articles written for this task. We will assess the articles across four dimensions: Comprehensiveness, Insight, Instruction Following, and Readability. The content is as follows:
<task>
"{task_prompt}"
</task>

**Articles to Evaluate**
<article_1>
"{article_1}"
</article_1>

<article_2>
"{article_2}"
</article_2>

**Evaluation Criteria**
Now, you need to evaluate and compare these two articles based on the following **evaluation criteria list**, providing comparative analysis and scoring each on a scale of 0-10. Each criterion includes an explanation, please understand carefully.

<criteria_list>
{criteria_list}
</criteria_list>

<Instruction>
**Your Task**
Please strictly evaluate and compare `<article_1>` and `<article_2>` based on **each criterion** in the `<criteria_list>`. You need to:
1.  **Analyze Each Criterion**: Consider how each article fulfills the requirements of each criterion.
2.  **Comparative Evaluation**: Analyze how the two articles perform on each criterion, referencing the content and criterion explanation.
3.  **Score Separately**: Based on your comparative analysis, score each article on each criterion (0-10 points).

**Scoring Rules**
For each criterion, score both articles on a scale of 0-10 (continuous values). The score should reflect the quality of performance on that criterion:
*   0-2 points: Very poor performance. Almost completely fails to meet the criterion requirements.
*   2-4 points: Poor performance. Minimally meets the criterion requirements with significant deficiencies.
*   4-6 points: Average performance. Basically meets the criterion requirements, neither good nor bad.
*   6-8 points: Good performance. Largely meets the criterion requirements with notable strengths.
*   8-10 points: Excellent/outstanding performance. Fully meets or exceeds the criterion requirements.

**Output Format Requirements**
Please **strictly** follow the `<output_format>` below for each criterion evaluation. **Do not include any other unrelated content, introduction, or summary**. Start with "Standard 1" and proceed sequentially through all criteria:
</Instruction>

<output_format>
{{
    "comprehensiveness": [
        {{
            "criterion": [Text content of the first comprehensiveness evaluation criterion],
            "analysis": [Comparative analysis],
            "article_1_score": [Continuous score 0-10],
            "article_2_score": [Continuous score 0-10]
}},
{{
            "criterion": [Text content of the second comprehensiveness evaluation criterion],
            "analysis": [Comparative analysis],
            "article_1_score": [Continuous score 0-10],
            "article_2_score": [Continuous score 0-10]
        }},
        ...
    ],
    "insight": [
        {{
            "criterion": [Text content of the first insight evaluation criterion],
            "analysis": [Comparative analysis],
            "article_1_score": [Continuous score 0-10],
            "article_2_score": [Continuous score 0-10]
        }},
        ...
    ],
    ...
}}
</output_format>

Now, please evaluate the two articles based on the research task and criteria, providing detailed comparative analysis and scores according to the requirements above. Ensure your output follows the specified `<output_format>` and that the JSON format is parsable, with all characters that might cause JSON parsing errors properly escaped.
</user_prompt>
"""

# Static version: uses predefined fixed evaluation criteria, no need to dynamically generate criteria
generate_static_score_prompt = """
<system_role>You are a strict, meticulous, and objective research article evaluation expert. You excel at using specific assessment criteria to deeply compare two articles on the same task, providing precise scores and clear justifications.</system_role>

<user_prompt>
**Task Background**
There is a deep research task, and you need to evaluate two research articles written for this task. We will assess the articles across four dimensions: Comprehensiveness, Insight, Instruction Following, and Readability. The content is as follows:
<task>
"{task_prompt}"
</task>

**Articles to Evaluate**
<article_1>
"{article_1}"
</article_1>

<article_2>
"{article_2}"
</article_2>

**Evaluation Criteria**
Now, you need to evaluate and compare these two articles based on the following **fixed evaluation criteria list**, providing comparative analysis and scoring each on a scale of 0-10. Each criterion includes an explanation, please understand carefully.

<criteria_list>
# Comprehensiveness
[
  {{
    "criterion": "Information Coverage Breadth",
    "explanation": "Evaluates whether the article covers all key areas and aspects related to the topic without omitting important information.",
    "weight": 0.25
  }},
  {{
    "criterion": "Information Depth and Detail",
    "explanation": "Evaluates whether the article provides sufficiently detailed information rather than just surface-level overviews.",
    "weight": 0.25
  }},
  {{
    "criterion": "Data and Factual Support",
    "explanation": "Evaluates whether the article provides sufficient data, facts, cases, or evidence to support its arguments and analysis.",
    "weight": 0.25

content.content:
<system_role>You are a professional article editor who is good at cleaning and refining article content.</system_role>

<user_prompt>
The input below may be a complete research article or just a continuous fragment (chunk) of one.
Operate only on the content provided; do not assume, complete, or quote any context outside this fragment.

Cleaning goal: remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2], 1, 2, etc. or other complex citation formats), reference lists, footnotes, and ensure the content reads smoothly.
Keep all other original content; remove only citations. If a citation mark wraps content that is part of a sentence, keep the text inside and drop only the marks.

Special case: if the entire input is a reference / bibliography / footnote-definition section of some article with no body text, return an empty string.

Input:
"{article}"

Return the cleaned text in full, without any additional explanation or comment.
</user_prompt>
